mn77:1.2: At one time the _Bhagavā_ was dwelling at Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the Squirrel Feeding Ground.
mn77:10.1: There are, Udāyī, five other qualities because of which my disciples respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me.
mn77:10.3: Here, Udāyī, my disciples hold me in high regard with respect to higher ethical conduct:
mn77:10.4: ‘The ascetic Gotama is ethical, possessed of a supreme aggregate of ethical conduct.’
mn77:11.1: In that, Udāyī, my disciples hold me in high regard with respect to higher ethical conduct:
mn77:11.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama is ethical, possessed of a supreme aggregate of ethical conduct,’ this is the first quality because of which my disciples respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me.
mn77:12.1: Again, Udāyī, my disciples hold me in high regard for outstanding knowledge and vision:
mn77:12.10: The ascetic Gotama teaches _dhamma_ from direct knowledge, not without direct knowledge.
mn77:12.11: The ascetic Gotama teaches _dhamma_ with its cause, not without cause.
mn77:12.12: The ascetic Gotama teaches _dhamma_ with a wonder, not without a wonder,’ this is the second quality because of which my disciples respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me.
mn77:12.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama says “I know” only when knowing;
mn77:12.3: the ascetic Gotama says “I see” only when seeing.
mn77:12.4: The ascetic Gotama teaches _dhamma_ from direct knowledge, not without direct knowledge.
mn77:12.5: The ascetic Gotama teaches _dhamma_ with its cause, not without cause.
mn77:12.6: The ascetic Gotama teaches _dhamma_ with a wonder, not without a wonder.’
mn77:12.7: In that, Udāyī, my disciples hold me in high regard for outstanding knowledge and vision:
mn77:12.8: ‘The ascetic Gotama says “I know” only when knowing;
mn77:12.9: the ascetic Gotama says “I see” only when seeing.
mn77:13.1: Again, Udāyī, my disciples hold me in high regard with respect to discernment [*paññāya*]:
mn77:13.10: ‘The ascetic Gotama is discerning [*paññavā*], possessed of a supreme aggregate of discernment [*paññākkhandhena*];
mn77:13.11: it is impossible that he would fail to see a future line of argument, or fail to refute with _dhamma_ a present doctrine of others when challenged.
mn77:13.13: This is the third quality, Udāyī, because of which my disciples respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me.
mn77:13.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama is discerning [*paññavā*], possessed of a supreme aggregate of discernment [*paññākkhandhena*];
mn77:13.3: it is impossible that he would fail to see a future line of argument, or fail to refute with _dhamma_ a present doctrine of others when it has been well challenged.’
mn77:13.5: Could my disciples, knowing and seeing in this way, keep throwing in comments from time to time?”
mn77:13.7: “And I, Udāyī, do not expect instruction from my disciples;
mn77:13.8: rather, my disciples expect instruction from me alone.
mn77:13.9: In that, Udāyī, my disciples hold me in high regard with respect to discernment [*paññāya*]:
mn77:14.1: Again, Udāyī, when my disciples are oppressed by dukkha, overcome by dukkha, they come to me and ask about the noble truth of dukkha; being asked, I explain to them the noble truth of dukkha, and I satisfy their mind by answering the question;
mn77:14.10: I satisfy their mind by answering the question.
mn77:14.11: This is the fourth quality, Udāyī, because of which my disciples respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me.
mn77:14.2: they ask me about the origin of dukkha …
mn77:14.4: they ask me about the noble truth of the way of practice leading to the cessation of dukkha; being asked, I explain to them the noble truth of the way of practice leading to the cessation of dukkha, and I satisfy their mind by answering the question.
mn77:14.5: In that, Udāyī, when my disciples are oppressed by dukkha, overcome by dukkha, they come to me and ask about the noble truth of dukkha, and being asked, I explain to them the noble truth of dukkha. I satisfy their mind by answering the question.
mn77:14.6: They ask me about the origin of dukkha …
mn77:14.8: they ask me about the noble truth of the way of practice leading to the cessation of dukkha.
mn77:14.9: Being asked, I explain to them the noble truth of the way of practice leading to the cessation of dukkha.
mn77:15.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples develop the four _satipaṭṭhānas_.
mn77:15.2: Here, Udāyī, a _bhikkhu_ dwells contemplating body in body, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with _sati_ [*satimā*], having removed desire and distress regarding the world;
mn77:15.4: he dwells contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*] …
mn77:15.5: he dwells contemplating _dhammas_ in _dhammas_, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with _sati_ [*satimā*], having removed desire and distress regarding the world.
mn77:15.6: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:16.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples develop the four right strivings.
mn77:16.2: Here, Udāyī, a _bhikkhu_ generates desire, makes an effort, arouses energy, steadies the mind [*citta*], and strives for the non-arising of bad, unskillful _dhammas_ that have not arisen;
mn77:16.3: he generates desire, makes an effort, arouses energy, steadies the mind [*citta*], and strives for the abandoning of bad, unskillful _dhammas_ that have arisen;
mn77:16.4: he generates desire, makes an effort, arouses energy, steadies the mind [*citta*], and strives for the arising of skillful _dhammas_ that have not arisen;
mn77:16.5: he generates desire, makes an effort, arouses energy, steadies the mind [*citta*], and strives for the stability, non-confusion, increase, expansion, development [*bhāvanāya*], and fulfillment of skillful _dhammas_ that have arisen.
mn77:16.6: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:17.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples develop the four bases of power.
mn77:17.2: Here, Udāyī, a _bhikkhu_ develops the basis of power endowed with _samādhi_ based on desire and the saṅkhāras of striving [*chandasamādhipadhānasaṅkhārasamannāgataṁ iddhipādaṁ*],
mn77:17.3: he develops the basis of power endowed with _samādhi_ based on energy and the saṅkhāras of striving [*vīriyasamādhipadhānasaṅkhārasamannāgataṁ iddhipādaṁ*],
mn77:17.4: he develops the basis of power endowed with _samādhi_ based on mind [*citta*] and the saṅkhāras of striving [*cittasamādhipadhānasaṅkhārasamannāgataṁ iddhipādaṁ*],
mn77:17.5: he develops the basis of power endowed with _samādhi_ based on investigation and the saṅkhāras of striving [*vīmaṁsāsamādhipadhānasaṅkhārasamannāgataṁ iddhipādaṁ*].
mn77:17.6: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:18.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples develop the five *indriyas*.
mn77:18.2: Here, Udāyī, a _bhikkhu_ develops the *indriya* of confidence [*saddhindriyaṁ*], leading to peace, leading to full awakening;
mn77:18.6: he develops the *indriya* of discernment [*paññindriyaṁ*], leading to peace, leading to full awakening.
mn77:18.7: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:19.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples develop the five *balas*.
mn77:19.2: Here, Udāyī, a _bhikkhu_ develops the *bala* of confidence [*saddhābalaṁ*], leading to peace, leading to full awakening;
mn77:19.6: he develops the *bala* of discernment [*paññābalaṁ*], leading to peace, leading to full awakening.
mn77:19.7: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:2.1: Now at that time several well-known wanderers were staying in the wanderers’ park at the Peacocks’ Feeding Ground, that is:
mn77:2.2: Annabhāra, Varadhara, the wanderer Sakuludāyī, and other well-known wanderers.
mn77:20.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples develop the seven *bojjhaṅgas*.
mn77:20.2: Here, Udāyī, a _bhikkhu_ develops the *bojjhaṅga* of _sati_ [*satisambojjhaṅgaṁ*], dependent on seclusion [*viveka*], dependent on fading away, dependent on cessation, ripening in relinquishment;
mn77:20.3: he develops the *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of _dhammas_ [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgaṁ*] …
mn77:20.8: he develops the *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅgaṁ*], dependent on seclusion [*viveka*], dependent on fading away, dependent on cessation, ripening in relinquishment.
mn77:20.9: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:21.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples develop the noble eightfold path.
mn77:21.2: Here, Udāyī, a _bhikkhu_ develops right view, develops right intention, develops right speech, develops right action, develops right livelihood, develops right effort, develops right _sati_, develops right _samādhi_.
mn77:21.3: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:22.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples develop the eight liberations.
mn77:22.10: completely transcending the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*], one enters and dwells in the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘Consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is infinite’:
mn77:22.12: completely transcending the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], one enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘There is nothing’:
mn77:22.14: completely transcending the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*], one enters and dwells in the dimension of neither *saññā* nor non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*]:
mn77:22.16: completely transcending the dimension of neither *saññā* nor non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*], one enters and dwells in the cessation of *saññā* and _vedanā_ [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*]:
mn77:22.18: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:22.4: perceiving what is formless inwardly, one sees forms outwardly:
mn77:22.6: one is intent only on the beautiful:
mn77:22.8: with the complete transcending of saññās of form [*rūpasaññānaṁ*], with the vanishing of saññās of resistance [*paṭighasaññānaṁ*], through non-attention to saññās of diversity [*nānattasaññānaṁ*], one enters and dwells in the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘Space is infinite’:
mn77:23.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples develop the eight dimensions of mastery [*abhibhāyatanāni*].
mn77:23.10: This is the third dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.11: Perceiving what is formless inwardly, one sees immeasurable forms outwardly, beautiful and ugly.
mn77:23.12: One is thus aware: ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.13: This is the fourth dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.14: Perceiving what is formless inwardly, one sees outwardly blue forms, blue in color, blue in appearance, blue in radiance.
mn77:23.15: Just as a flax flower is blue, blue in color, blue in appearance, blue in radiance; or like a cloth from Benares, polished on both sides, blue, blue in color, blue in appearance, blue in radiance;
mn77:23.16: so too, perceiving what is formless inwardly, one sees outwardly blue forms, blue in color, blue in appearance, blue in radiance.
mn77:23.17: One is thus aware: ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.18: This is the fifth dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.19: Perceiving what is formless inwardly, one sees outwardly yellow forms, yellow in color, yellow in appearance, yellow in radiance.
mn77:23.2: Perceiving form inwardly, one sees limited forms outwardly, beautiful and ugly.
mn77:23.20: Just as a yellow trumpet flower is yellow, yellow in color, yellow in appearance, yellow in radiance; or like a cloth from Benares, polished on both sides, yellow, yellow in color, yellow in appearance, yellow in radiance;
mn77:23.21: so too, perceiving what is formless inwardly, one sees outwardly yellow forms, yellow in color, yellow in appearance, yellow in radiance.
mn77:23.22: One is thus aware: ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.23: This is the sixth dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.24: Perceiving what is formless inwardly, one sees outwardly red forms, red in color, red in appearance, red in radiance.
mn77:23.25: Just as a bandhujīvaka flower is red, red in color, red in appearance, red in radiance; or like a cloth from Benares, polished on both sides, red, red in color, red in appearance, red in radiance;
mn77:23.26: so too, perceiving what is formless inwardly, one sees outwardly red forms, red in color, red in appearance, red in radiance.
mn77:23.27: One is thus aware: ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.28: This is the seventh dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.29: Perceiving what is formless inwardly, one sees outwardly white forms, white in color, white in appearance, white in radiance.
mn77:23.3: One is thus aware: ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.30: Just as the morning star is white, white in color, white in appearance, white in radiance; or like a cloth from Benares, polished on both sides, white, white in color, white in appearance, white in radiance;
mn77:23.31: so too, perceiving what is formless inwardly, one sees outwardly white forms, white in color, white in appearance, white in radiance.
mn77:23.32: One is thus aware: ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.33: This is the eighth dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.34: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:23.4: This is the first dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.5: Perceiving form inwardly, one sees immeasurable forms outwardly, beautiful and ugly.
mn77:23.6: One is thus aware: ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.7: This is the second dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.8: Perceiving what is formless inwardly, one sees limited forms outwardly, beautiful and ugly.
mn77:23.9: One is thus aware: ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:24.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples develop the ten dimensions of totality [*kasiṇāyatanāni*].
mn77:24.11: one perceives the consciousness-totality [*viññāṇakasiṇaṁ*] above, below, and across, non-dual, immeasurable.
mn77:24.12: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:24.2: One perceives the earth-totality [*pathavīkasiṇaṁ*] above, below, and across, non-dual, immeasurable;
mn77:25.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples develop the four *jhānas*.
mn77:25.2: Here, Udāyī, a _bhikkhu_, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful _dhammas_, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*].
mn77:25.3: He drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with the *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*], so that there is no part of his whole body not pervaded by the *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*].
mn77:25.4: Just as, Udāyī, a skilled bath attendant or apprentice would pour bath powder into a bronze dish and knead it, sprinkling it again and again with water, so that the ball of bath powder, saturated, soaked through, and pervaded within and without with moisture, would yet not drip;
mn77:25.5: so too, Udāyī, a _bhikkhu_ drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with the *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*], so that there is no part of his whole body not pervaded by the *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*].
mn77:26.1: Again, Udāyī, with the settling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with internal confidence … he enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*.
mn77:26.2: He drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with the *pīti* and *sukha* born of _samādhi_, so that there is no part of his whole body not pervaded by the *pīti* and *sukha* born of _samādhi_.
mn77:26.3: Just as, Udāyī, there were a deep lake with water welling up from below. It would have no inflow of water from the east, no inflow of water from the west, no inflow of water from the north, no inflow of water from the south, and the sky would not from time to time properly send down showers of rain;
mn77:26.4: yet from that very lake a cool spring would well up and drench, steep, fill, and pervade that very lake with cool water, so that there would be no part of the whole lake not pervaded by cool water.
mn77:26.5: So too, Udāyī, a _bhikkhu_ drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with the *pīti* and *sukha* born of _samādhi_, so that there is no part of his whole body not pervaded by the *pīti* and *sukha* born of _samādhi_.
mn77:27.1: Again, Udāyī, with the fading away of *pīti* … he enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*.
mn77:27.2: He drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *sukha* devoid of *pīti*, so that there is no part of his whole body not pervaded by *sukha* devoid of *pīti*.
mn77:27.3: Just as, Udāyī, in a lotus pond or a pond of blue lotuses or white lotuses, some blue lotuses or lotuses or white lotuses, born in the water, grown in the water, not rising above the water, would thrive immersed in the water; from tip to root they would be drenched, steeped, filled, and pervaded with cool water, so that there would be no part of those blue lotuses or lotuses or white lotuses not pervaded by cool water;
mn77:27.4: so too, Udāyī, a _bhikkhu_ drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *sukha* devoid of *pīti*, so that there is no part of his whole body not pervaded by *sukha* devoid of *pīti*.
mn77:28.1: Again, Udāyī, with the abandoning of *sukha* and the abandoning of dukkha, and with the earlier disappearance of joy and sorrow, a _bhikkhu_ enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither painful nor pleasant and has purity of _sati_ through equanimity [*upekkhā*].
mn77:28.2: He sits, having suffused this very body with a pure, bright mind, so that there is no part of his whole body not pervaded by the pure, bright mind.
mn77:28.3: Just as, Udāyī, a man might sit wrapped from head to foot in a white cloth, so that there would be no part of his whole body not pervaded by the white cloth;
mn77:28.4: so too, Udāyī, a _bhikkhu_ sits, having suffused this very body with a pure, bright mind, so that there is no part of his whole body not pervaded by the pure, bright mind.
mn77:28.5: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:29.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples understand thus:
mn77:29.10: ‘This body of mine is material, made of the four great elements, produced by mother and father, built up by rice and porridge, subject to impermanence, rubbing, crushing, breaking, and destruction;
mn77:29.11: and this consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] of mine is supported here and bound up here.’
mn77:29.12: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:29.2: ‘This body of mine is material, made of the four great elements, produced by mother and father, built up by rice and porridge, subject to impermanence, rubbing, crushing, breaking, and destruction;
mn77:29.3: and this consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] of mine is supported here and bound up here.’
mn77:29.4: Just as, Udāyī, there might be a beryl gem, beautiful, of pure water, eight-faceted, well worked, clear, transparent, and possessing every excellence;
mn77:29.5: and through it were strung a thread that was blue or yellow or red or white or brown.
mn77:29.6: A sharp-sighted person, taking it in his hand, might examine it thus:
mn77:29.7: ‘This is a beryl gem, beautiful, of pure water, eight-faceted, well worked, clear, transparent, and possessing every excellence;
mn77:29.8: and through it is strung a thread that is blue or yellow or red or white or brown.’
mn77:29.9: So too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples understand thus:
mn77:3.1: Then, in the morning, the _Bhagavā_ dressed, took his bowl and robe, and entered Rājagaha for alms.
mn77:3.3: “It is still too early to wander for alms in Rājagaha.
mn77:3.4: What if I were to go to the wanderers’ park at the Peacocks’ Feeding Ground, to the wanderer Sakuludāyī?”
mn77:30.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples create from this body another body, material, mind-made, complete in all its parts, not lacking any faculty.
mn77:30.10: ‘This is the snake, this is the basket; the snake is one thing, the basket another; yet the snake has been taken out of the basket.’
mn77:30.11: So too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples create from this body another body, material, mind-made, complete in all its parts, not lacking any faculty.
mn77:30.12: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:30.2: Just as, Udāyī, a man might draw a reed out from its sheath;
mn77:30.4: ‘This is the sheath, this is the reed; the sheath is one thing, the reed another; yet the reed has been drawn out from the sheath.’
mn77:30.5: Or just as, Udāyī, a man might draw a sword from its scabbard;
mn77:30.7: ‘This is the sword, this is the scabbard; the sword is one thing, the scabbard another; yet the sword has been drawn out from the scabbard.’
mn77:30.8: Or just as, Udāyī, a man might pull a snake out from a basket;
mn77:31.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples experience the various kinds of supernormal power: being one, they become many; being many, they become one; appearance and disappearance; they go unhindered through walls, ramparts, and mountains as if through space; they dive in and out of the earth as if it were water; they walk on water without sinking as if it were earth; seated cross-legged, they travel through the sky like a winged bird; with the hand they touch and stroke even the moon and sun, so mighty and powerful; they exercise mastery with the body even as far as the Brahmā world.
mn77:31.2: Just as, Udāyī, a skilled potter or potter’s apprentice, with well-prepared clay, could make and produce whatever kind of vessel he might wish;
mn77:31.3: or just as, Udāyī, a skilled ivory-worker or ivory-worker’s apprentice, with well-prepared ivory, could make and produce whatever kind of ivory object he might wish;
mn77:31.4: or just as, Udāyī, a skilled goldsmith or goldsmith’s apprentice, with well-prepared gold, could make and produce whatever kind of golden object he might wish;
mn77:31.5: so too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples experience the various kinds of supernormal power: being one, they become many; being many, they become one; appearance and disappearance; they go unhindered through walls, ramparts, and mountains as if through space; they dive in and out of the earth as if it were water; they walk on water without sinking as if it were earth; seated cross-legged, they travel through the sky like a winged bird; with the hand they touch and stroke even the moon and sun, so mighty and powerful; they exercise mastery with the body even as far as the Brahmā world.
mn77:31.6: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:32.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples hear both kinds of sounds with the divine ear-element, purified and surpassing the human — divine as well as human, those far and near.
mn77:32.2: Just as, Udāyī, a strong conch-blower could easily make himself heard in the four directions;
mn77:32.3: so too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples hear both kinds of sounds with the divine ear-element, purified and surpassing the human — divine as well as human, those far and near.
mn77:32.4: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:33.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples understand the mind [*ceto*] of other beings, of other persons, having encompassed it with their own mind [*cetasā*]:
mn77:33.10: they understand an expanded mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘expanded mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.11: they understand an unexpanded mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘unexpanded mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.12: they understand a surpassable mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘surpassable mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.13: they understand an unsurpassable mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘unsurpassable mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.14: they understand a mind [*cittaṁ*] endowed with _samādhi_ as ‘mind [*cittaṁ*] endowed with _samādhi_’;
mn77:33.15: they understand a mind [*cittaṁ*] not endowed with _samādhi_ as ‘mind [*cittaṁ*] not endowed with _samādhi_’;
mn77:33.16: they understand a liberated mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘liberated mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.17: they understand an unliberated mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘unliberated mind [*cittaṁ*]’.
mn77:33.18: Just as, Udāyī, a woman or a man, young, youthful, fond of adornment, examining the reflection of their own face in a clean, bright mirror or in a clear bowl of water, would know, if there were a blemish, ‘there is a blemish,’ or if there were no blemish, ‘there is no blemish’;
mn77:33.19: so too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples understand the mind [*ceto*] of other beings, of other persons, having encompassed it with their own mind [*cetasā*]:
mn77:33.2: they understand a lustful mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘lustful mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.20: they understand a lustful mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘lustful mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.21: they understand a mind [*cittaṁ*] free from lust …
mn77:33.3: they understand a mind [*cittaṁ*] free from lust as ‘mind [*cittaṁ*] free from lust’;
mn77:33.35: they understand an unliberated mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘unliberated mind [*cittaṁ*]’.
mn77:33.36: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:33.4: they understand an angry mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘angry mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.5: they understand a mind [*cittaṁ*] free from anger as ‘mind [*cittaṁ*] free from anger’;
mn77:33.6: they understand a deluded mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘deluded mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.7: they understand a mind [*cittaṁ*] free from delusion as ‘mind [*cittaṁ*] free from delusion’;
mn77:33.8: they understand a contracted mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘contracted mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.9: they understand a scattered mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘scattered mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:34.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples recollect their manifold past abodes, that is: one birth, two births, three births, four births, five births, ten births, twenty births, thirty births, forty births, fifty births, a hundred births, a thousand births, a hundred thousand births, many eons of contraction, many eons of expansion, many eons of contraction and expansion: ‘There I was named such-and-such, of such-and-such clan, of such-and-such appearance, such was my food, such my experience of *sukha* and dukkha, such the limit of my life; passing away from there, I arose elsewhere; and there too I was named such-and-such, of such-and-such clan, of such-and-such appearance, such was my food, such my experience of *sukha* and dukkha, such the limit of my life; passing away from there, I arose here.’ Thus they recollect their manifold past abodes in their modes and details.
mn77:34.2: Just as, Udāyī, a man might go from his own village to another village, and from that village go to yet another village, and from that village return again to his own village. It would occur to him: ‘I went from my own village to another village; there I stood thus, sat thus, spoke thus, and kept silent thus. From that village I went to that other village; there too I stood thus, sat thus, spoke thus, and kept silent thus. Now I have returned from that village to my own village.’
mn77:34.3: So too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples recollect their manifold past abodes, that is: one birth … thus they recollect their manifold past abodes in their modes and details.
mn77:34.4: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:35.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples, with the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, see beings passing away and being reborn, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good destination and a bad destination, and they understand beings as going according to their kamma: ‘These beings, indeed, engaged in bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct, revilers of the noble ones, with wrong view, undertaking actions based on wrong view — with the breakup of the body, after death, have arisen in a plane of misery, a bad destination, a lower world, hell. But these beings, engaged in bodily good conduct, verbal good conduct, and mental good conduct, not revilers of the noble ones, with right view, undertaking actions based on right view — with the breakup of the body, after death, have arisen in a good destination, a heavenly world.’ Thus, with the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, they see beings passing away and being reborn, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good destination and a bad destination, and they understand beings as going according to their kamma.
mn77:35.2: Just as, Udāyī, there might be a house with two doors. A sharp-sighted person standing in the middle could see people entering the house, leaving it, walking to and fro, and moving about;
mn77:35.3: so too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples, with the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, see beings passing away and being reborn, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good destination and a bad destination, and understand beings as going according to their kamma …
mn77:35.4: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:36.1: Again, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples dwell having directly known and realized for themselves, in this very life, the liberation of mind without _āsavas_ and the liberation by discernment [*paññā*], through the destruction of the _āsavas_.
mn77:36.2: Just as, Udāyī, on a mountain shelf there might be a lake, clear, transparent, and untroubled. A sharp-sighted person standing on the bank might see shells and gravel and shoals of fish moving about and standing still in it. It would occur to him: ‘This lake is clear, transparent, and untroubled, and here there are shells and gravel and shoals of fish, moving about and standing still.’
mn77:36.3: So too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path of practice, and practicing in accordance with it, my disciples dwell having directly known and realized for themselves, in this very life, the liberation of mind without _āsavas_ and the liberation by discernment [*paññā*], through the destruction of the _āsavas_.
mn77:36.4: And among them many of my disciples dwell having reached the consummation and perfection of direct knowledges.
mn77:37.1: This, Udāyī, is the fifth quality because of which my disciples respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me.
mn77:38.1: These, Udāyī, are the five qualities because of which my disciples respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me.”
mn77:38.3: Satisfied, the wanderer Sakuludāyī delighted in what the _Bhagavā_ had said.
mn77:38.4: The Greater Discourse to Sakuludāyī is finished, the seventh.
mn77:4.1: Then the _Bhagavā_ went to the wanderers’ park at the Peacocks’ Feeding Ground.
mn77:4.10: Perhaps, seeing an assembly that is quiet, he may think it fit to approach.”
mn77:4.2: Now at that time the wanderer Sakuludāyī was sitting together with a large assembly of wanderers, noisy, loud, making a great din, engaged in many kinds of low talk, such as talk about kings, talk about thieves, talk about ministers, talk about armies, talk about dangers, talk about battles, talk about food, talk about drink, talk about clothes, talk about beds, talk about garlands, talk about perfumes, talk about relatives, talk about vehicles, talk about villages, talk about market towns, talk about cities, talk about countries, talk about women, talk about heroes, talk about streets, talk about wells, talk about those departed long ago, assorted talk, tales about the world, tales about the sea, and talk of being and non-being.
mn77:4.4: The wanderer Sakuludāyī saw the _Bhagavā_ coming from afar.
mn77:4.5: Seeing him, he quieted his own assembly:
mn77:4.7: do not make noise, good sirs.
mn77:4.9: that venerable one likes quiet and speaks in praise of quiet.
mn77:5.1: Then the _Bhagavā_ went up to the wanderer Sakuludāyī.
mn77:5.10: When the wanderer Sakuludāyī was sitting to one side, the _Bhagavā_ said this to him:
mn77:5.11: “For what discussion are you sitting together now, Udāyī? And what was your conversation that was left unfinished in the middle?”
mn77:5.2: Then the wanderer Sakuludāyī said this to the _Bhagavā_:
mn77:5.5: It has been a long time, bhante, since the _Bhagavā_ found an occasion to come here.
mn77:5.8: The _Bhagavā_ sat down on the prepared seat.
mn77:5.9: And the wanderer Sakuludāyī too took a certain low seat and sat down to one side.
mn77:6.1: “Let that discussion be, bhante, for which we are sitting together now.
mn77:6.12: Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta is also a leader of a community, a leader of a group, a teacher of a group, known, famous, a founder of a sect, regarded as good by many people;
mn77:6.13: he too has settled down in Rājagaha for the rains.
mn77:6.14: And this ascetic Gotama too is a leader of a community, a leader of a group, a teacher of a group, known, famous, a founder of a sect, regarded as good by many people;
mn77:6.15: he too has settled down in Rājagaha for the rains.
mn77:6.16: Now which of these venerable ascetics and brahmins — these leaders of communities, leaders of groups, teachers of groups, known, famous, founders of sects, regarded as good by many people — is respected, esteemed, honored, and revered by his disciples? And relying on whom do the disciples dwell, after respecting and esteeming him?’
mn77:6.18: ‘This Pūraṇa Kassapa is a leader of a community, a leader of a group, a teacher of a group, known, famous, a founder of a sect, regarded as good by many people;
mn77:6.19: but he is not respected, esteemed, honored, or revered by his disciples, nor do his disciples dwell relying on Pūraṇa Kassapa after respecting and esteeming him.
mn77:6.2: That discussion will not be difficult for the _Bhagavā_ to hear later as well.
mn77:6.20: Formerly Pūraṇa Kassapa was teaching _dhamma_ to an assembly of many hundreds.
mn77:6.21: Then one disciple of Pūraṇa Kassapa made a noise:
mn77:6.22: “Do not ask Pūraṇa Kassapa about this matter, good sirs;
mn77:6.24: We know this matter; ask us about it,
mn77:6.25: and we will explain it to you, good sirs.”
mn77:6.26: Formerly Pūraṇa Kassapa, raising his arms, weeping, could not get him to stop:
mn77:6.27: “Let the good sirs be quiet, do not make noise, good sirs.
mn77:6.28: They are not asking you, venerables; they are asking me.
mn77:6.29: I will explain it to them.”
mn77:6.3: In recent days, bhante, on an earlier day, various ascetics and brahmins of different sects had sat together and assembled in the debating hall, and this topic came up in the course of conversation:
mn77:6.30: But many of Pūraṇa Kassapa’s disciples, after attacking him in debate, left, saying:
mn77:6.31: “You do not understand this _dhamma_ and _vinaya_ [*dhammavinayaṁ*]; I understand this _dhamma_ and _vinaya_ [*dhammavinayaṁ*]. How could you understand this _dhamma_ and _vinaya_ [*dhammavinayaṁ*]? You are practicing wrongly; I am practicing rightly. What is coherent with me is incoherent with you. What should have been said first you said afterward; what should have been said afterward you said first. What you have long worked out has been turned inside out. Your doctrine has been refuted, you are defeated. Go and try to rescue your thesis, or disentangle yourself if you can.”
mn77:6.32: Thus Pūraṇa Kassapa is not respected, esteemed, honored, or revered by his disciples, nor do his disciples dwell relying on Pūraṇa Kassapa after respecting and esteeming him.
mn77:6.33: And Pūraṇa Kassapa has been abused with a censure in the name of _dhamma_.’
mn77:6.39: Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta is also a leader of a community, a leader of a group, a teacher of a group, known, famous, a founder of a sect, regarded as good by many people;
mn77:6.4: ‘It is a gain indeed for the Aṅgans and Magadhans, sirs, a great gain indeed for the Aṅgans and Magadhans.
mn77:6.40: but he is not respected, esteemed, honored, or revered by his disciples, nor do his disciples dwell relying on Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta after respecting and esteeming him.
mn77:6.41: Formerly Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta was teaching _dhamma_ to an assembly of many hundreds.
mn77:6.42: Then one disciple of Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta made a noise:
mn77:6.43: “Do not ask Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta about this matter, good sirs;
mn77:6.45: We know this matter; ask us about it,
mn77:6.46: and we will explain it to you, good sirs.”
mn77:6.47: Formerly Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, raising his arms, weeping, could not get him to stop:
mn77:6.48: “Let the good sirs be quiet, do not make noise, good sirs.
mn77:6.49: They are not asking you, venerables; they are asking me.
mn77:6.5: For these ascetics and brahmins who are leaders of communities, leaders of groups, teachers of groups, known, famous, founders of sects, regarded as good by many people, have settled down in Rājagaha for the rains.
mn77:6.50: I will explain it to them.”
mn77:6.51: But many of Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta’s disciples, after attacking him in debate, left, saying:
mn77:6.52: “You do not understand this _dhamma_ and _vinaya_ [*dhammavinayaṁ*]; I understand this _dhamma_ and _vinaya_ [*dhammavinayaṁ*]. How could you understand this _dhamma_ and _vinaya_ [*dhammavinayaṁ*]? You are practicing wrongly. I am practicing rightly. What is coherent with me is incoherent with you; what should have been said first you said afterward, what should have been said afterward you said first. What you have long worked out has been turned inside out. Your doctrine has been refuted, you are defeated. Go and try to rescue your thesis, or disentangle yourself if you can.”
mn77:6.53: Thus Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta is not respected, esteemed, honored, or revered by his disciples, nor do his disciples dwell relying on Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta after respecting and esteeming him.
mn77:6.54: And Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta has been abused with a censure in the name of _dhamma_.’
mn77:6.56: ‘This ascetic Gotama too is a leader of a community, a leader of a group, a teacher of a group, known, famous, a founder of a sect, regarded as good by many people;
mn77:6.57: and he is respected, esteemed, honored, and revered by his disciples, and his disciples dwell relying on the ascetic Gotama after respecting and esteeming him.
mn77:6.58: Formerly the ascetic Gotama was teaching _dhamma_ to an assembly of many hundreds.
mn77:6.59: Then one disciple of the ascetic Gotama coughed.
mn77:6.6: This Pūraṇa Kassapa too is a leader of a community, a leader of a group, a teacher of a group, known, famous, a founder of a sect, regarded as good by many people;
mn77:6.60: Another fellow practitioner nudged him with his knee:
mn77:6.61: “Let the venerable one be quiet; do not make noise, venerable one. Our teacher, the _Bhagavā_, is teaching _dhamma_.”
mn77:6.62: At the time when the ascetic Gotama teaches _dhamma_ to an assembly of many hundreds, then there is not even the sound of a sneeze or the sound of a cough among his disciples.
mn77:6.63: And the great crowd stands there expectant:
mn77:6.64: “Whatever _dhamma_ the _Bhagavā_ will speak, that we shall hear.”
mn77:6.65: Just as if a man were to press clean honey at a crossroads,
mn77:6.66: and a great crowd would stand around him expectantly,
mn77:6.67: so too, at the time when the ascetic Gotama teaches _dhamma_ to an assembly of many hundreds, then there is not even the sound of a sneeze or the sound of a cough among his disciples.
mn77:6.68: And the great crowd stands there expectant:
mn77:6.69: “Whatever _dhamma_ the _Bhagavā_ will speak, that we shall hear.”
mn77:6.7: he too has settled down in Rājagaha for the rains.
mn77:6.70: Even those disciples of the ascetic Gotama who, having been urged on by fellow practitioners, renounce the training and return to a lesser life, they too speak in praise of the Teacher, speak in praise of the _dhamma_, and speak in praise of the _saṅgha_; they blame only themselves, not others: “We ourselves were unlucky, we had little merit, we who went forth in such a well-proclaimed _dhamma_ and _vinaya_ [*dhammavinaye*] were unable to live the complete and perfectly pure holy life for as long as life lasts.”
mn77:6.71: Having become attendants in monasteries or lay followers, they undertake and practice the five training rules.
mn77:6.72: Thus the ascetic Gotama is respected, esteemed, honored, and revered by his disciples, and his disciples dwell relying on the ascetic Gotama after respecting and esteeming him.’”
mn77:7.1: “But how many qualities, Udāyī, do you see in me, in the _dhamma_, because of which my disciples respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me?”
mn77:8.1: “I see five qualities, bhante, in the _Bhagavā_, because of which the _Bhagavā_ is respected, esteemed, honored, and revered by his disciples, who dwell relying on him after respecting and esteeming him.
mn77:8.10: Because the _Bhagavā_, bhante, is content with whatever lodging there is, and speaks in praise of contentment with whatever lodging there is, this is the fourth quality I see in the _Bhagavā_ because of which his disciples respect him, esteem him, honor him, and revere him, and dwell relying on him after respecting and esteeming him.
mn77:8.11: Again, bhante, the _Bhagavā_ is secluded [*pavivitto*], and he speaks in praise of seclusion [*pavivekassa*].
mn77:8.12: Because the _Bhagavā_, bhante, is secluded [*pavivitto*], and speaks in praise of seclusion [*pavivekassa*], this is the fifth quality I see in the _Bhagavā_ because of which his disciples respect him, esteem him, honor him, and revere him, and dwell relying on him after respecting and esteeming him.
mn77:8.13: These are the five qualities I see in the _Bhagavā_, bhante, because of which his disciples respect him, esteem him, honor him, and revere him, and dwell relying on him after respecting and esteeming him.”
mn77:8.3: The _Bhagavā_, bhante, eats little, and he speaks in praise of eating little.
mn77:8.4: Because the _Bhagavā_, bhante, eats little, and speaks in praise of eating little, this is the first quality I see in the _Bhagavā_ because of which his disciples respect him, esteem him, honor him, and revere him, and dwell relying on him after respecting and esteeming him.
mn77:8.5: Again, bhante, the _Bhagavā_ is content with whatever robe there is, and he speaks in praise of contentment with whatever robe there is.
mn77:8.6: Because the _Bhagavā_, bhante, is content with whatever robe there is, and speaks in praise of contentment with whatever robe there is, this is the second quality I see in the _Bhagavā_ because of which his disciples respect him, esteem him, honor him, and revere him, and dwell relying on him after respecting and esteeming him.
mn77:8.7: Again, bhante, the _Bhagavā_ is content with whatever almsfood there is, and he speaks in praise of contentment with whatever almsfood there is.
mn77:8.8: Because the _Bhagavā_, bhante, is content with whatever almsfood there is, and speaks in praise of contentment with whatever almsfood there is, this is the third quality I see in the _Bhagavā_ because of which his disciples respect him, esteem him, honor him, and revere him, and dwell relying on him after respecting and esteeming him.
mn77:8.9: Again, bhante, the _Bhagavā_ is content with whatever lodging there is, and he speaks in praise of contentment with whatever lodging there is.
mn77:9.1: “If, Udāyī, my disciples were to respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me, because ‘the ascetic Gotama eats little and speaks in praise of eating little,’ there are, Udāyī, my disciples who eat but a bowlful, half a bowlful, a bael fruit, or half a bael fruit.
mn77:9.10: If, Udāyī, my disciples were to respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me, because ‘the ascetic Gotama is content with whatever lodging there is and speaks in praise of contentment with whatever lodging there is,’ there are, Udāyī, my disciples who dwell at the root of a tree and in the open air; for eight months they do not go under a roof.
mn77:9.11: But I, Udāyī, sometimes dwell in peaked houses, plastered within and without, sheltered from the wind, with well-fitted door-bars and shuttered windows.
mn77:9.12: If, Udāyī, my disciples were to respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me, because ‘the ascetic Gotama is content with whatever lodging there is and speaks in praise of contentment with whatever lodging there is,’ then those disciples of mine who dwell at the root of a tree and in the open air, who for eight months do not go under a roof, would not respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, or dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me because of this quality.
mn77:9.13: If, Udāyī, my disciples were to respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me, because ‘the ascetic Gotama is secluded [*pavivitto*] and speaks in praise of seclusion [*pavivekassa*],’ there are, Udāyī, my disciples who are forest dwellers, living in remote lodgings, inhabiting remote forest and woodland resting places; they come down into the midst of the _saṅgha_ every half-month for the recitation of the Pātimokkha.
mn77:9.14: But I, Udāyī, sometimes dwell crowded with _bhikkhus_, _bhikkhunis_, laymen, laywomen, kings, royal ministers, followers of other sects, and disciples of other sects.
mn77:9.15: If, Udāyī, my disciples were to respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me, because ‘the ascetic Gotama is secluded [*pavivitto*] and speaks in praise of seclusion [*pavivekassa*],’ then those disciples of mine who are forest dwellers, living in remote lodgings, inhabiting remote forest and woodland resting places, and who come down into the midst of the _saṅgha_ every half-month for the recitation of the Pātimokkha, would not respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, or dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me because of this quality.
mn77:9.16: Thus, Udāyī, it is not because of these five qualities that my disciples respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me.
mn77:9.2: But I, Udāyī, sometimes eat a bowl filled to the brim, and even more.
mn77:9.3: If, Udāyī, my disciples were to respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me, because ‘the ascetic Gotama eats little and speaks in praise of eating little,’ then those disciples of mine who eat but a bowlful, half a bowlful, a bael fruit, or half a bael fruit would not respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, or dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me because of this quality.
mn77:9.4: If, Udāyī, my disciples were to respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me, because ‘the ascetic Gotama is content with whatever robe there is and speaks in praise of contentment with whatever robe there is,’ there are, Udāyī, my disciples who wear rag-robes, who wear rough robes; they gather discarded rags from charnel grounds, rubbish heaps, or shops, make them into an outer robe, and wear it.
mn77:9.5: But I, Udāyī, sometimes wear householders’ robes, sturdy, coarse, and hairy like gourd-fiber.
mn77:9.6: If, Udāyī, my disciples were to respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me, because ‘the ascetic Gotama is content with whatever robe there is and speaks in praise of contentment with whatever robe there is,’ then those disciples of mine who wear rag-robes, who wear rough robes, who gather discarded rags from charnel grounds, rubbish heaps, or shops, make them into an outer robe, and wear it, would not respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, or dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me because of this quality.
mn77:9.7: If, Udāyī, my disciples were to respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me, because ‘the ascetic Gotama is content with whatever almsfood there is and speaks in praise of contentment with whatever almsfood there is,’ there are, Udāyī, my disciples who live on alms gathered house to house, devoted to the observance of gleaning, and even when they have entered among houses, if invited to a seat they do not consent.
mn77:9.8: But I, Udāyī, sometimes eat invited meals of rice from fine grain, well cleaned, with many soups and many dishes.
mn77:9.9: If, Udāyī, my disciples were to respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, and dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me, because ‘the ascetic Gotama is content with whatever almsfood there is and speaks in praise of contentment with whatever almsfood there is,’ then those disciples of mine who live on alms gathered house to house, devoted to the observance of gleaning, and who, when they have entered among houses, do not consent even when invited to a seat, would not respect me, esteem me, honor me, and revere me, or dwell relying on me after respecting and esteeming me because of this quality.
